coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent N,S,P tri-doped carbon dots for Cl− detection and their assistance of TiO2 as the catalyst in the degradation of methylene blue
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Lu-Shuang
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: The preparation and application exploration of multi-heteroatom doped carbon dots (CDs) have drawn tremendous attention. In this study, the N, S, P tri-doped carbon dots (NSP-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with sulfonamide and phosphoric acid as precursors, possessing a fluorescence quantum yield up to 33 %. The fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs exhibited a good linear relationship with the surrounding pH in the range of 3–7, showing the great promising pH sensing potential of NSP-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence of NSP-CDs could be selectively quenched by Cl−; based on it, a Cl− fluorescence sensor was constructed and successfully applied to detecting Cl− in sweat samples with the recoveries of 94 %–110 %. Furthermore, TiO2 was modified with the NSP-CDs by simple immersion method, and the as-prepared composite demonstrated higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methylene blue under UV irradiation than pristine TiO2. The prepared NSP-CDs had potential for multiple applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-heteroatom doping
Chloride ion
Sensor
Photodegradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cl− using NSP-CDs as the probe

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of preparation conditions, (a) weight of sulfanilamide, (b) reaction temperature and (c) reaction time, on QYNSP-CDs.
TEM image (a) and size distribution (b) of NSP-CDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum of NSP-CDs. (b) XPS full scan spectrum of NSP-CDs. (c-g) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, P 2p and S 2p.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NSP-CDs. Inset in (a): photographs of NSP-CDs under visible light (left) and UV light of 365 nm (right). (b) Excitation spectrum (Ex, λem = 480 nm) and emission spectra of NSP-CDs with different λex. (c) Fluorescence spectra of NSP-CDs at different temperature. (d) Photostability of NSP-CDs.

(a) The images of NSP-CDs solutions in PBS at different pHs under visible (up) and 365 nm of UV (down) light. Fluorescence spectra of NSP-CDs solutions in different pHs regulated by (b) PBS, (c) HAcO/NaAcO and (d) BR. (e) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs solution and pH values.

Influence of (a) pH, (b) concentration of NSP-CDs and (c) reaction time on detection of Cl−. (d) The selectivity of NSP-CDs for Cl− over other ions and the interference of them (competition). (e) Fluorescence spectra of the NSP-CDs solution with different concentrations of Cl−. (f) The linear relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Cl−.

(a) MB degradation rate at different irradiation time with different TiO2-NSP-CDs. (b) Recycle performance of the TiO2-NSP-CDs. (c) XRD patterns of TiO2, original TiO2-NSP-CDs, and TiO2-NSP-CDs after 4 recycling experiments.
Detection of Cl− in simulated sweat.
Comparison of different CDs modified TiO2 for catalytic degradation of MB.

row.entry: Degradation efficiency (using TiO2 as the catalyst)
Degradation efficiency (using CDs modified TiO2 as the catalyst)

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *increased ratio = Degradation efficiency (using CDs modified TiO2 as the catalyst)/ Degradation efficiency (using TiO2 as the catalyst).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent N,S,P tri-doped carbon dots for Cl− detection and their assistance of TiO2 as the catalyst in the degradation of methylene blue

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Life and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education
School of Life and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Tropical Biological Resources of Ministry of Education, School of Life and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation and application exploration of multi-heteroatom doped carbon dots (CDs) have drawn tremendous attention. In this study, the N, S, P tri-doped carbon dots (NSP-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with sulfonamide and phosphoric acid as precursors, possessing a fluorescence quantum yield up to 33 %. The fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs exhibited a good linear relationship with the surrounding pH in the range of 3–7, showing the great promising pH sensing potential of NSP-CDs. Moreover, the fluorescence of NSP-CDs could be selectively quenched by Cl−; based on it, a Cl− fluorescence sensor was constructed and successfully applied to detecting Cl− in sweat samples with the recoveries of 94 %–110 %. Furthermore, TiO2 was modified with the NSP-CDs by simple immersion method, and the as-prepared composite demonstrated higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methylene blue under UV irradiation than pristine TiO2. The prepared NSP-CDs had potential for multiple applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), emerging carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, are extensively applied in wide fields, owing to their diversified attractive properties, like small size, high water solubility, low toxicity, benign biocompatibility, photostability and ease of surface functionalization [1–8]. Much effort has been devoted to developing the highly fluorescent CDs with desirable properties for multiple applications. Doping is an efficient approach to enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs, as different elements can adjust the surface states of CDs and provide diverse emissive sites [1,9–11]. Especially, the multi-heteroatom doping is in some cases more effective to tune the physical and chemical properties of CDs [12,13], extending the applicability of CDs. For examples, the fluorescence of S and N co-doped CDs prepared with water chestnut and onion was quenched by Cu(II) and then restored by coenzyme A, based on which an off-on fluorescence probe for high sensitivity determination of coenzyme A was constructed [14]. Wang et al. fabricated the N and Cl co-doped CDs using a choline chloride-glycerine deep eutectic solvent, showing pH-sensitive fluorescent emission and obvious fluorescence response to cytochrome c [15]. The fluorescent probes for manganese(VII) and l-ascorbic acid were established based on the N, S, P co-doped CDs fabricated with Saccharomyces cerevisiae [13].

Cl− is an essential electrolyte that maintains body fluid and ion homeostasis. The change of Cl− concentration levels in biological fluids may indicate various abnormal conditions and the occurrence of diseases [16]. Cystic fibrosis (CF) is a genetic disease caused by mutations in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator gene presented in a variety of organs. Elevated Cl− concentration in swear (>60 mM) is the gold standard for diagnosis of CF [17,18]. However, most of the existing Cl− detection methods, like ion exchange chromatography, ion-selective electrodes and colorimetry, cannot meet the high-performance and low-cost clinical needs. Hence, it is of great importance to develop a selective, sensitive, rapid and low-cost method for Cl− determination. To the best of our knowledge, few studies about the fluorescence sensor for the determination of Cl− in sweat were reported.

Photocatalytic degradation is a commonly-adopted method for wastewater treatment [19–21]. Thanks to the advantages of low toxicity, chemical inertness, high catalytic efficiency and low cost, TiO2 nanoparticles were the most popular catalyst for photocatalytic degradation [22]. However, TiO2 had some disadvantages, such as electron-hole recombination, wide band gap energy, high concentration aggregation, low specific surface area, etc., which limited the degradation efficiency. Carbonaceous substance modification on the surface of TiO2 was documented as a solution to these issues [23–26]. Since the introduced carbonaceous substance could act as the binding center of electron-hole pairs, the photoexcited electrons in TiO2 may be transferred to carbonaceous substance thus hindering the recombination of electrons and holes and enhancing the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 [23–26]. As far as CDs are concerned, they are less than 10 nm, and more edges and defects are present on the surface. Additionally, different functional groups or chemical structures is beneficial to the adsorption and reduction of oxygen. Therefore, CDs is expected to enhance the catalytic ability of TiO2.

In the present study, the N, S, and P tri-doped CDs (NSP-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with sulfonamide and phosphoric acid as precursors. The fluorescence of NSP-CDs was sensitive to the concentration of Cl−, which was adopted to determine Cl− in sweat. Meanwhile, NSP-CDs can also enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for the degradation of methylene blue under UV irradiation.

Sulfanilamide, H3PO4, NaOH, H2SO4, H3BO3, K2SO4, Na2SO4, CaSO4, MgSO4, (NH4)2SO4, HAcO, NaAcO, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, Na3PO4, NaCl, KBr, KI, glucose, citric acid and methylene blue (MB) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was produced by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). Aeroxide TiO2 P25 was received from Evonik Degussa Corporation (Frankfurt, Germany).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were captured by a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) spectra were recorded using an AVATAR 360 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) in the wavenumber range of 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). UV–vis spectra were obtained using a Lambda 365 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). An F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) was used for fluorescence measurement. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out by a TG 209F1 Libra thermal analyzer (Netzsch, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

Sulfonamide (0.1 g) was ultrasonic dissolved in 5 mL H3PO4, and the solution was subjected to 220 °C for 10 h in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel. After cooling down to the room temperature, the resultant solution was diluted with ultra-pure water of 5 times volume, neutralized with NaOH, and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 MWCO) for 12 h with renewing the ultrapure water every 1 h to remove unreacted small molecules. Afterwards, by rotary evaporation, the NSP-CDs solid was obtained and dried in the oven for further use.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of NSP-CDs (QYNSP-CDs) was measured with quinine sulfate (QY of 54 % at 360 nm) as the reference. A series of quinine sulfate and NSP-CDs solutions were prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index η = 1.33) and ultrapure water (η = 1.33), respectively, with the absorbance of each solution below 0.1 to minimize the self-absorption effect. The absorbance and fluorescence measurements were performed at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The QYNSP-CDs was calculated by the following Eq. (1),
(1) QY = QYR·(k/kR)·(η/ηR)2
where QY represents the quantum yield, k is the gradient of the plot with integrated fluorescence intensity as a function of absorbance, η is the refractive index of the solvent and the subscript R refers to reference (quinine sulfate).

The spectrometric properties of NSP-CDs were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence measurement. To evaluate the stability of NSP-CDs in response to temperature, the fluorescence spectra of NSP-CDs solution was monitored at different temperatures (20 °C–65 °C). The fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs was measured at different time intervals upon the continuous irradiation with the UV light of 360 nm to assess the photostability.

The pH values of the NSP-CDs solutions ranging 2–10 were regulated with phosphate buffer saline (PBS), HAcO/NaAcO and Britton-Robinson (BR) buffers, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of these solutions were then recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cl− using NSP-CDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: For Cl− detection, NaCl was used as a representative to obtain the stock solution of Cl−. The working solutions of Cl− were prepared by diluting the stock solution with ultrapure water to a series of concentrations (0, 0.2 mM, 0.5 mM, 1 mM, 2 mM, 5 mM, 10 mM, 20 mM, 50 mM, 100 mM). For the typical Cl− detection process, the pH of the Cl− standard working or sample solution was adjusted to 3 with H2SO4. Then 50 μL NSP-CDs solution (60 μg/mL) was added into the above solution of 2 mL and homogeneously mixed. After incubation at room temperature for 10 min, the solution was for the fluorescence intensity measurement. The quenching efficiency (F0/F) was calculated, where F0 was the fluorescence intensity of the blank solution (without Cl−) and F was the fluorescence intensity of the sample solution (with Cl−).

The commonly-encountered coexisting ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, NH4 +, OH−, AcO−, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, Br−, I−) or small molecules (glucose, citric acid) were selected to evaluate the selectivity of NSP-CDs. 50 μL NSP-CDs solution was introduced into the sample solution of these individual substances at the concentration of 50 mM, and then for fluorescence intensity measurement. In addition, for evaluating the interfering effects, each of the above substances (50 mM) was introduced into the Cl− (50 mM) standard working solution, and the fluorescence response to Cl− was recorded. For all the above studies, the fluorescence intensity was measured at the excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths of 330 nm and 410 nm, respectively.

To verify the feasibility of the method, the simulated human sweat was analyzed only after 10 times dilution with ultrapure water. The Cl− spiked simulated sweat samples were analyzed to calculate the recovery as the following Eq. (2),
(2) Recovery (%)=(Cmeasured/(Cadded+C0))×100 %
where Cmeasured is the concentration measured by the fluorescence sensor, C0 is the original concentration of Cl− in simulated human sweat, and Cadded stands for the spiked concentration of Cl−.

TiO2 (200 mg) was ultrasonically dispersed in 12 mL NSP-CDs solutions (10 μg/mL) and shaken at 60 rpm and 25 °C for 1 h. After centrifugation, the TiO2-NSP-CDs were collected, washed with ultrapure water and then dried at 80 °C for further use.

Photocatalytic degradation studies were performed with a low-pressure mercury lamp (20 W, λ =254 nm). MB was chosen as a model organic dye pollutant. The catalyst (50 mg) was ultrasonically dispersed into 100 mL MB aqueous solution with an initial concentration of 10 μg/mL. The suspension was stirred in dark for 2 h to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the dye and the catalyst, and then irradiated with UV light. At the irradiation intervals of 0, 20 min, 40 min, 60 min, 80 min, 100 min, 120 min and 150 min, 2 mL suspension was drawn out and centrifuged rapidly (12,000 r/min, 10 min). The supernatant was then subjected to UV absorbance measurement at 665 nm. Photodegradation rate of MB was calculated according to the change of the absorbance value.

After a single catalytic test, TiO2-NSP-CDs in the suspension were collected by centrifugation, washed with water for several times and dried at 80 °C. The recycled TiO2-NSP-CDs (50 mg) were used for the next photocatalytic degradation procedure as described above. This procedure was repeated 4 times and the photodegradation rates of MB with the photocatalytic time were compared to evaluate the reusability of the TiO2-NSP-CDs.

In this study, sulfanilamide with both abundant nitrogen and sulfur was for the first time chosen as the carbon source to prepare carbon dots, in addition to use phosphoric acid as the phosphorus source. After the hydrothermal reaction, the pale brown solution was obtained, giving out bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation. In order to achieve the high fluorescence quantum yield, the amount of sulfonamide, the hydrothermal temperature and the reaction time were optimized successively with the volume of phosphoric acid fixing at 5 mL. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), QYNSP-CDs first increased and then decreased with the increasing amount of sulfonamide maximizing at 0.10 g of it. Although the exact mechanism was unclear, the NSP-CDs possessing different proportions of elements corresponding to the different amounts of reactants may have different structure and surface luminescent groups, and thus different fluorescent performance. The hydrothermal temperature and reaction time were then optimized with the amount of sulfonamide at 0.10 g. As depicted in Fig. 1(b), as the reaction temperature increased from 160 °C to 200 °C, the QYNSP-CDs increased from 18 % to 33 %, demonstrating positive effect of temperature on QYNSP-CDs in this temperature range. As the reaction temperature further went up to 220 °C, the QYNSP-CDs reduced to 17 %. The similar relationship was also observed between the QYNSP-CDs and reaction time, reaching a maximum value at 12 h. According to previous reports [27], only high enough temperature and long enough reaction time can guarantee the sufficient carbonization of the precursor, forming CDs and achieving high QY. On the contrary, much higher reaction temperature and too longer reaction time may destroy the emissive sites which have formed on the surface, thus causing the reduce of QY [28]. In summary, the optimum synthesis conditions are as follows: 5 mL phosphoric acid, 0.10 g sulfanilamide, 200 °C and 6 h. Under the optimal reaction condition, the QYNSP-CDs was measured to be 33 %.
The morphology and size distribution of NSP-CDs were observed by TEM in Fig. 2 (a). NSP-CDs are uniformly monodispersed particles with a size distribution of 2 nm–5 nm.

FTIR and XPS characterizations were used to disclose the chemical composition and surface states of NSP-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), the FTIR absorption bands were assigned as follows: 3423 cm−1 (ʋO–H and ʋN–H), 2931 cm−1 (ʋC–H), 2355 cm−1 (ʋO=C=O), 1636 cm−1 (ʋC=C/C=O) and 1168 cm−1 (ʋP=O) [29–31].

The XPS full scan spectrum of NSP-CDs is depicted in Fig. 3(b). Five prominent peaks at 284.73 eV, 532.09 eV, 401.63 eV, 168.02 eV and 134.03 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p, and P 2p, respectively, are observed [29–32]. The elemental composition of NSP-CDs is 26.44 % C, 55.09 % O, 3.34 % N, 1.71 % S and 13.41 % P. From the result, though the N and S were co-introduced into the NSP-CDs from sulfanilamide, the ratio of N and S in NSP-CDs was different from the ratio of them in the sulfanilamide molecule. This phenomenon was also noticed in many previous reports [33–35]. The element composition in CDs may not be identical to that of their precursors ascribing to the complicated formation processes, including polymerization, cyclization, re-organization, pyrolysis, carbonization and so on [1], which may result in the production of some by-products. Although the doped content of N and S in NSP-CDs was not very high, a higher content of P was obtained, which contributed much to the fluorescence enhancement of CDs. In our preliminary experiment, the carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal method with sole sulfanilamide showed very weak fluorescence, while the fluorescence was obviously increased with the addition of P. According to FTIR and high resolution XPS spectra, there are a variety of heteroatoms (O, N, S, and P) contained functional groups on the NSP-CDs, which could be beneficial to the solubility and stability of NSP-CDs in water and provide acting sites for subsequent applications.

Subsequently, the optical properties of the NSP-CDs were studied in detail. As photographed in Fig. 4 (a), the NSP-CDs solution was transparent and yellowish under sunlight and emitted strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation. A strong absorption at 255 nm and a weak absorption at 360 nm were observed, which can be attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of functional groups, respectively [28]. Fig. 4(b) is the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NSP-CDs solution. The maximum excitation wavelength was 360 nm which was consistent with the weak absorption peak in the UV absorption spectrum. It could be speculated that the ultraviolet absorption at 360 nm may be produced by the fluorescence emitting groups [36]. With the varied excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 400 nm, the maximum emission wavelength of 480 nm remained unchanged but with the varied fluorescence intensity, indicating no excitation-dependent emission behavior and the good mono-dispersion of NSP-CDs.

The fluorescence stability is of vital importance in practical applications which was investigated in terms of varied temperature and light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 4(c), the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs decreased slightly with the elevated temperature, retaining 80 % of the highest fluorescence intensity even if the temperature reached 65 °C. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs solution remained almost consistent upon continuous irradiation at 360 nm for 60 min in Fig. 4(d). These results indicate that the NSP-CDs possessed both excellent thermal and photo stability.

In our preliminary experiment, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs was sensitive to the environmental pH. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), with excitating at 365 nm using UV lamp, the change of the fluorescence intensity of the NSP-CDs solution at different pHs could be observed with the naked eye. The pH response behavior of NSP-CDs was then investigated in detail. As depicted in Fig. 5(b, c, d), a gradually decreased fluorescence intensity was observed along with the increasing pH values from 3 to 7 for all the three studied buffers, i.e. PBS, HAcO/NaAcO and BR. The results confirmed that NSP-CDs had high sensitivity to pH irrelevant to the buffer type. The pH-dependent photoluminescence properties of NSP-CDs may be related to a large number of acidic or basic functional groups on their surface, such as amino, hydroxyl and and carboxyl groups, etc. Protonation and deprotonation of these groups occurred depending on the surrounding pHs, leading to the change of the surface properties of NSP-CDs; hence, it affected the non-radiative recombination rate of electron-hole pairs, thus causing the change of fluorescence intensity [15].

As depicted in Fig. 5(e), good linear relationships between the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs and pHs (3–7) adjusted with three different buffers were obtained. The three lines almost overlapped, which further illustrated the identical response to the pHs of 3−7. In addition, the correlation coefficients (R2) of the regression equations were larger than 0.99. A potential application of NSP-CDs as a fluorescence nanoprobe for pH sensing was highly expected.

Cl− was found to effectively quench the fluorescence of NSP-CDs, and thus NSP-CDs was investigated for sensing Cl−. To obtain a better fluorescence response of the probe, the detection conditions, including pH, the concentration of NSP-CDs, and the incubation time, were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 6 (a), a higher quenching efficiency was achieved under acidic condition than alkaline and neutral conditions. Also considering acidity was favorable for NSP-CDs fluorescence, the probe system was conducted at pH 3. As displayed in Fig. 6(b), the NSP-CDs concentration had little effect on the fluorescence quenching efficiency within the investigated range. Considering the precision of the instrument and less consumption of NSP-CDs, the concentration of NSP-CDs was fixed at 60 μg/mL. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs responded to Cl− very rapidly and then remained stable within 30 min. This quick and constant response was beneficial to rapid detection. In summary, the optimum conditions for detecting Cl− are as follows: the concentration of NSP-CDs was 60 μg/mL, the pH value of the sample solution was adjusted to 3 with H2SO4 and the incubation time was 1 min at room temperature.

Under the optimal detection conditions, the selectivity of the method and the interference from other potential coexisting substances were tested. As depicted in Fig. 6(d), F0/F remained almost constant except in the presence of I− and Cl−, indicating the high selectivity of this probe. In addition, the co-existing substances almost had no influence on the detection of Cl− except I−. Considering that the concentration of I− in sweat was much lower than Cl−, the effect of I− on the detection of Cl− in sweat could be ignored. The above results suggested that NSP-CDs are promising candidate probes for detecting Cl− in sweat.

As shown in Fig. 6 (e), the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs decreased with the increasing Cl− concentration. The Stern-Volmer equation (3) was used to analyze the quenching process,
(3) F0/F-1=Ksv[C]
F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs without and with the Cl−, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [C] is the concentration of the Cl−. A good linearity is observed with Ksv = 0.0419 and R2 = 0.99.

As depicted in Fig. 6(f), the linear relationship between F0/F and Cl− concentration was observed within the range of 0.2 mM–100 mM, covering 60 mM which is the sign of elevated sweat chloride indicating the probability of the existence of CF [16]. Therefore, NSP-CDs could be used in the diagnosis of this disease. The detection limit of Cl− was calculated to be 0.06 mM. The established method was successfully applied to the direct determination of Cl− concentration in simulated sweat. As listed in Table 1 , recoveries over the range of 94 %–110 % with RSDs at 2.8 %–6.1 % were obtained, suggesting the accuracy and reliability of this assay.

The multi heteroatom doping and the excellent fluorescence performance made the NSP-CDs potentially applicable as co-catalysis. Herein, the complex of TiO2 and NSP-CDs, i.e. TiO2-NSP-CDs, was investigated as the catalyst for UV degradation of MB. As compared in Fig. 7 (a), without the photocatalyst or with the addition of the single NSP-CDs, no significant degradation of MB was observed upon UV-light irradiation, which demonstrated that NSP-CDs alone had no catalytic degradation effect. Obviously, the TiO2-NSP-CDs showed higher degradation performance than the pristine TiO2. After 40 min UV irradiation, 90 % of MB was degraded in the presence of TiO2-NSP-CDs, which was about 39 % higher than that of TiO2. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of TiO2-NSP-CDs may be due to the synergistic effect of TiO2 nanoparticles and NSP-CDs. The heteroatom-contained functional groups in NSP-CDs can produce high-density redox-active centers and rich edges and defects. The photoexcited electrons in TiO2 may be transferred to NSP-CDs, thus hindering the recombination of electrons with holes, which further enhanced the catalytic ability of the material [22,26,37]. As compared in Table 2 , the degradation efficiency of MB using CDs modified TiO2 as the catalyst was increased to different degrees than pristine TiO2. Compared with the reported studies, the proposed TiO2-NSP-CDs had moderate increased ratio, demonstrating NSP-CDs showed a moderate ability on the improvement of catalytic ability of TiO2.

The recyclability of photocatalysts is an essential issue for practical utilization. Owing to strong interactions between CDs and TiO2, possibly like Van der Waals' force, electrostatic interactions, and even some chemical bonds, TiO2-NSP-CDs were stable enough for the recycling catalysis. The results displayed in Fig. 7(b) revealed that the TiO2-NSP-CDs demonstrated a satisfactory recyclability in terms of stable catalytic degradation rate and efficiency after 4 cycles of recycling. Furthermore, the TiO2, TiO2-NSP-CDs and the TiO2-NSP-CDs after four catalytic cycles were characterized by XRD (Fig. 7(c)). The peak positions in these patterns matched well with those for standard TiO2 anatase, and the peaks were intense and sharp indicating the crystallinity of TiO2-NSP-CDs remained after recycling. TiO2-NSP-CDs was a high-efficiency catalyst for the degradation of MB showing practical application potential in industrial wastewater treatment.

The N,S,P tri-doped carbon dots (NSP-CDs) were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal method with sulfonamide and phosphoric acid as precursors. Under optimal conditions (0.10 g sulfanilamide dissolved in 5 mL phosphoric acid was heated at 220 °C for 10 h), the NSP-CDs achieved a high fluorescence quantum yield up to 33 %. The particularly multi-heteroatom-doping made the fluorescence intensity of NSP-CDs sensitive to pH and Cl−. The Cl− probe based on NSP-CDs was successively applied in sweat samples. NSP-CDs can also enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for the degradation of methylene blue under UV irradiation.
Lu-Shuang Li: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Li Xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2172019KFYRCPY112) and Starting Research Fund from the Hainan University (No. KYQD(ZR)19106).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging, sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Du, S. Guo, Chemically doped fluorescent carbon and graphene quantum dots for bioimaging, sensor, catalytic and photoelectronic applications, Nanoscale 8(5) (2016) 2532-2543.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications
J. Mater. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, Z. Kang, Y. Liu, S.-T. Lee, Carbon nanodots: synthesis, properties and applications, J. Mater. Chem. 22(46) (2012) 24230.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots from Guar Gum: synthesis, characterization and preliminary in vivo application in plant cells
Mat Sci Eng B-Solid

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Bajpai, A. D'Souza, B. Suhail, Carbon dots from Guar Gum: Synthesis, characterization and preliminary in vivo application in plant cells, Mat Sci Eng B-Solid 241 (2019) 92-99.
